mnemonics.content: Shared Memory Attachment Process: GET a segment, ATTACH it to address space, USE it, DETACH when done, CONTROL if needed
mnemonics.title: Shared Memory Lifecycle

acronyms.usage: General term for mechanisms allowing processes to communicate
Creates or obtains shared memory segment identifier
Attaches shared memory to process address space
Detaches shared memory from process address space
Performs control operations on shared memory segment
Maps files or anonymous memory to process address space
Generates unique key from pathname for IPC identification
Flag for mmap making modifications visible to other processes

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a whiteboard in a common area: multiple people can walk up and read/write directly without passing notes through a messenger (kernel). The whiteboard is the shared memory, processes are people, messenger is kernel for pipe communication.
Think of shared memory as a file folder and shmat() as giving each process their own desk copy of that folder - they all reference the same original but work from their own space.
Imagine two people trying to write on the same paper at once - they'll overwrite each other. The semaphore is like a 'occupied' sign that prevents simultaneous writes.

analogies.analogy: Shared memory is like a shared refrigerator where everyone can directly grab food. Pipes are like having to ask a waiter to bring food from the kitchen - more overhead but organized.
mmap() is like putting a new cover on a book - whatever you write on the cover (memory) gets associated with the book's content (file). Changes to cover reflect in the book.
ftok() generating a key is like a unique room number. Anyone with the room number (key) can find and access that specific shared space.
Semaphores act like a ticket dispenser at a service counter - only N customers (processes) can access the service (shared resource) at once.
